A “features + benefits” approach explains the features of the products in the context of how those features benefit the user, and a little aspirational romance copy helps your customers see themselves using your product. Put it all together in second person (“you”) makes it clear you’re speaking directly to your customer — and they like that, which is a boon for customer retention.

Product Photography

Would you ever consider using your driver's license photo to represent you on your website? Hells no. Most of the time, DMV photos come out looking like mugshots even if you say "cheese" with max enthusiasm.

The same conditions that lead to those embarrassing ID photos — poor lighting, thoughtless angle, hideous background, lack of creativity, and mastery of the camera — matter when it comes to your product photos. 

What it does & why you need it

Product photography can assist with the buying process and make or break conversion rates for eCommerce websites. After product descriptions, they are one of the most important factors for determining whether or not visitors purchase from you or a competitor. Great images also have the chance to be shared on Pinterest, Instagram and other social media sites, giving you more ways to reach your target market.

Pro tip: Our product photography service allows whatever you're selling to appear on your website pages in the best possible way. Our professional photographers know what type of lighting works. They're experts at displaying merchandise in an appealing way and will take the time to capture all of the important details so that your buyers can clearly see what it is they're buying.

Buying Guides

As an expert in whatever you're selling, you understand the features and benefits of your products — but your customers likely lack that expertise. Buying guides enable your customers to make informed buying decisions and move to checkout with confidence. 

Buying guides can be short ‘n’ sweet, or they can be comprehensive, but the idea is the same: break down what customers need to consider when buying the types of products that you sell, and make it as easy as possible for the reader to make the exact right choice.

What they do & why you need them

Buying guides define unfamiliar terms and answer common questions about a particular product or product type. Depending on your offerings, a buying guide can help customers compare similar items or envision all of the ways in which a product can improve their lives. Buying guides can be inspirational, aspirational, or cut-and-dry, laying out all the facts someone might need to know in order to make the best purchasing decision.

As long as they’re truly helpful to your customers, buying guides on your site can lead to increased conversions, since your shoppers are less likely to feel like they need to do more research elsewhere before they buy. Buying guides help lower return rates by ensuring that shoppers consider important aspects of your products. From an SEO standpoint, buying guides can help your store get discovered by people who Google terms like "how to choose a television" or "buying skis."

Below is an example of the different buying guides the surf brand Quicksilver has on its site to help consumers choose the perfect item for their needs.

Pro tip: Before you write a buying guide, determine who your audience is, what questions they might want answers to, and what your brand’s overarching value props are. Use product images and videos to enhance your buying guides, and optimize your guides with relevant keywords, keeping search intent top-of-mind. Stretch Creative can help you create helpful, engaging buying guides that give your audience all of the information they need to choose the product that’s right for them, converting to sales — and increasing brand loyalty.

Local City Pages

Once upon a time in a land where the Internet didn't yet exist, there was a book of knowledge called the Yellow Pages. Brave messengers deposited these mystical tomes on the doorsteps of businesses and homes. Those fortunate enough to receive one of these books were granted the ability to quickly find every plumber, pet supply store, attorney, and other businesses serving their local community. The Internet has made those hallowed yellow pages of old obsolete — now, people can simply type in a product or service and their city to get a list of stores or providers. 

If you have a brick-and-mortar store with a well-optimized website, your business is likely to appear close to the top when someone searches for the city included in your address. But what if they search the next town over? There's a chance that your site might appear organically — but it might not. And that’s where local city pages come in.

What they do & why you need them:

Local city pages help the odds be ever in your favor. These optimized web landing pages outline your products and services and include relevant content that references your city and those nearby.

A local city page is a geographic-themed landing page that improves your chances of showing up at the top of the SERP — if it’s helpful. In a world without yellow pages, optimized local city pages with excellent copywriting are the best way to ensure people can find your business throughout the region you serve — even if it’s a large metro area. 

When they’re authentically helpful and well-written and properly optimized, local city pages can increase emails, phone calls, and both web and foot traffic to your business, giving you more leads for sales.

Below is an example of a city page for CVS Pharmacy:

Pro tip: City page copy doesn’t need to be very long, but it should include relevant city-specific content and offer helpful information for those searching for a particular product or service in that location. Stretch Creative writes high-performing city landing pages with customers in mind. We create location content that makes a great first impression and clearly spells out why people in a certain city need to choose your business.

Email Copy

How often do you open and read all of the retailer emails you signed up for? If you’re like most, it’s probably not very often. According to Mail Chimp, overall, around 16% of recipients open emails from eComm brands. If you’re selling vitamins and supplements, around 15% of recipients open your emails. If you’re a beauty and personal care brand, around 17% of recipients open your emails. If you’re hawking electronics, it’s around 19%. 

But that doesn't mean email marketing isn't worth your time — emails can still lead to sales if they’re well-written. 

What it does & why you need it

Email copywriting gives you another way to communicate directly with both your existing customers and potential buyers. You can use emails to announce new products, advertise sales, keep your customers engaged, and even educate your customers about related topics of interest to them. 

Even if your weekly or monthly emails remain unopened, the recipients will still regularly see your name, promoting brand recognition. Great subject lines can help you convey your value props and further cement yourself in the minds of your prospects.

Once the recipient clicks into the email, they should find something worthwhile — helpful information, neat tips ‘n’ tricks, a discount code, or other beneficial messaging.

Below is a screen recording of a spectacularly done year-end email from Shein. It’s got tons of pictures, scores of deals, abundant shopping links, snackable copy that’s 100% on-brand, and plenty of fun opps for customer engagement.

Pro tip: Effective emails have a curiosity-sparking subject line and highly engaging content — and at Stretch Creative, we know how to write the kind that entices clicks rather than deletions and solidifies your brand through voice, imagery, and user experience.

User-Generated Content

User-generated content (UGC) is exactly what it sounds like — content created by users of your product. Many consumers routinely seek out UGC in their online community while shopping to help inform what they buy, whether their community lives on TikTok, Insta, or the eComm website itself. 

User-generated content on your website may include product reviews, testimonials, videos, guest-written blog posts, customer stories, and other content created by real people who use your product.

What it does & why you need it

User-generated content is a helpful human standing next to the cheese case waiting to give advice on which goes best with a chardonnay. It reinforces the benefits you tout in your product descriptions and helps your potential buyers feel more confident about the products they purchase.

Including user-generated eCommerce content on your site can also strengthen your SEO game. Search engines often prioritize sites that feature product reviews over those that don't. Properly optimized UGC might even appear in the search results when someone searches for reviews for a specific product or product category.

Our writers LOVE creating first-person content based on their experiences, as we've done for brands like Grove Collaborative, below. (Our writer took the photos, and our graphic design department did the illustrations!):

Pro tip: While it’s unethical to pay for positive product reviews, it’s another thing entirely to hire blog writers to offer candid, honest reviews of the products you carry.

Future-proof your eCommerce content

Staying ahead in ecommerce means thinking beyond the now and creating content that can adapt as trends, technologies, and consumer expectations evolve. Future-proofing your content isn’t about predicting every change—it’s about building flexibility and resilience into your strategy.

Here’s how to make sure your ecommerce content holds up in 2025 and beyond:

  • Optimize for new search behaviors: Search is no longer just keywords and queries. Voice search, visual search, and AI-assisted tools like ChatGPT are changing how consumers find products. Make sure your content is optimized for conversational queries and includes clear, engaging visuals that stand out in search results.
  • Focus on evergreen content: While it’s tempting to lean into trends, balance this with evergreen content that stays relevant over time. Think comprehensive guides, how-tos, and resources that offer value regardless of what’s trending.
  • Embrace sustainability: Shoppers are becoming more conscious of sustainability. Highlight your eco-friendly practices—whether it’s carbon-neutral shipping, recyclable packaging, or ethically sourced materials—and make these values clear in your content.
  • Leverage new formats: Consumers want content that fits into their daily lives. Short-form videos, live-stream shopping, and AR/VR product experiences are growing fast. Experiment with these formats to make your content more interactive and engaging.
  • Build trust through transparency: Trust is everything in eCommerce. Share behind-the-scenes content, show customer reviews, and create detailed FAQ sections to address common concerns.

By focusing on content that’s adaptable, trustworthy, and designed for evolving consumer habits, you’ll set your brand up for long-term success.

New trends and technologies shaping eCommerce content

Staying competitive in ecommerce means knowing how to use the latest tools and techniques to improve how customers find, interact with, and trust your brand. Here are some of the trends and technologies driving the next wave of ecommerce content:

  • AI-driven personalization: AI tools are making it easier to deliver personalized shopping experiences. Whether it’s generating product descriptions at scale, tailoring product recommendations, or creating dynamic email campaigns, AI helps brands connect with customers in meaningful ways without added effort.
  • Voice and visual search: Shoppers are turning to voice assistants and image-based search to find what they need. Optimizing your content to match natural language queries and ensuring high-quality, searchable visuals can help your products stand out on these platforms.
  • Augmented reality (AR) and virtual reality (VR): AR and VR let customers “try before they buy,” making online shopping more interactive. From virtual dressing rooms to AR furniture placement, this technology bridges the gap between in-store and online experiences.
  • Short-form video and live shopping: Platforms like TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube are transforming how people shop. Short videos and live-stream shopping events create engaging, real-time connections with audiences while driving sales.
  • Sustainability storytelling: Today’s customers want to know where their products come from and how they’re made. Highlighting sustainable practices through your content—like ethical sourcing or eco-friendly packaging—can build trust and loyalty.

Adapting these tools into your content strategy isn’t about chasing trends—it’s about meeting your customers where they are and creating an experience that feels intuitive, helpful, and worth returning to.

Need help with eCommerce content in 2025?

The content you put on the internet matters — a solid, consistent web presence is everything in the eCommerce world. The quality of writing matters, the tone of voice matters, and the value the reader walks away with matters.

Maybe you have a teeny tiny marketing team — or no marketing department at all — and need support with blog articles and social posts. Or maybe you’re a ginormous enterprise in need of hundreds of category pages. Our content creation process is highly collaborative and personalized, whether we create content ad hoc or at scale.

We’re a full-service content agency with a roster of talented creators including writers, designers, photographers, and video content producers. We’re experts in SEO and content strategy, and we know how to engage consumers, move them through the buying journey, and compel them to ADD TO CART.

Contact us today — we've got your content waiting.
excerpt: We spend a lot of time working with eCommerce brands—mid-sized to enterprise—with all this experience we wanted to share with you the ultimate guide to eCommerce content.
author: Kristen Bailey

title: Generative Engine Optimization: What is it and What do you Need to Know?
content:
What if your content wasn’t just discoverable but indispensable to AI-driven tools like ChatGPT? Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) makes this possible by bridging the gap between traditional SEO and the future of AI-powered search, ensuring your content doesn’t just get found—it gets used. In this guide, you’ll discover how to leverage GEO to enhance your digital strategy and gain a competitive edge in this new era of search.

What is Generative Engine Optimization (GEO)?

So, what exactly is GEO, and why should you care? Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) is the secret sauce that helps your content shine in AI-powered tools like ChatGPT. Think of it as the art of making sure your content isn’t just found by these tools, but actively used to craft those snappy, informative answers everyone loves.

Core Principles of Generative Engine Optimization:

The principles behind GEO are what make it a unique and necessary strategy for AI-driven search engines. These principles focus on adapting traditional SEO approaches, enhancing content for contextual understanding, and redefining the goals of optimization in this new era of search. Here’s a closer look:

Built on SEO Foundations

GEO isn’t a complete departure from SEO—it builds on the same core principles, like creating quality content and using strategic keywords. The difference lies in how it adapts these practices for AI engines. Instead of focusing solely on ranking factors like backlinks and metadata, GEO emphasizes the structure and clarity of content, ensuring it’s easily understood and utilized by generative models.

Contextual Optimization

This principle focuses on making your content not just readable but usable for AI systems. Imagine a recipe for chocolate chip cookies: instead of a long-winded story about your childhood love for cookies, you organize the content with clear sections—ingredients, steps, tips—so even a rushed reader can grab what they need quickly. AI engines love this kind of structured, purposeful content because it helps them pull exactly the right information to craft helpful, concise responses for users.

New Content Goals

With GEO, the goal shifts from simply ranking high on search engines to becoming a trusted resource for AI engines. Imagine your content as a go-to reference in a classroom—not just there to be read, but to be quoted and cited as the best answer to the question at hand. This means creating content that’s not just optimized for algorithms but is rich, engaging, and offers real value, so AI engines see it as worth referencing in their responses. It’s about meeting user intent with precision and seamlessly fitting into AI-driven answers that feel complete and authoritative.

The Evolution of Search: From Traditional Search Engines to AI-Driven

How do you stay ahead when search is evolving faster than ever? Traditional engines like Google may still dominate, but AI-powered tools like ChatGPT are rewriting the rules. Instead of just ranking pages, they’re curating answers—and this shift is reshaping how we create and optimize content. To compete, businesses need to understand and adapt to these changes before they get left behind.

Traditional Search Engines

For decades, traditional search engines like Google have been the foundation of online discovery, relying on methods like crawling and indexing to organize the web. These engines use algorithms to rank content by evaluating factors such as keyword optimization, link quality, and metadata.

For example, a search for “best hiking boots” might prioritize a website with well-placed keywords, authoritative backlinks, and descriptive metadata, while also showcasing featured snippets or ads to provide immediate answers. This systematic approach has shaped how we access information but is now being challenged by AI-driven alternatives.

AI-Driven Search Engines

AI-driven search engines like ChatGPT and Perplexity are revolutionizing how users access information. Unlike traditional search engines that display a list of links, these tools generate direct, conversational responses by synthesizing vast amounts of data into cohesive answers tailored to specific queries.

For example, imagine someone asks, “How do I start a vegetable garden?” Instead of linking to a collection of gardening blogs, the AI provides a concise guide: prepare your soil, choose plants suited to your climate, and establish a watering schedule. These steps are derived from credible, structured content that the AI can easily interpret and reformat.

This transformation demands that content creators rethink how they craft information. To ensure your content is utilized by AI, it needs to be purposefully designed, highly structured, and rich in value. This means creating resources that AI tools see as indispensable—content that answers questions thoroughly, is easy to interpret, and consistently stands out as the best available option.

How Generative AI Engines Work

Generative AI engines, like OpenAI’s GPT or Google’s Bard, are reshaping how users access information by turning complex queries into clear, synthesized answers. These systems rely on advanced machine learning to process data efficiently and deliver relevant results.

  • Data Aggregation: AI collects information from a vast range of sources, including websites, databases, and publications. For example, when asked about "how to save for retirement," it pulls insights from financial blogs, statistical reports, and expert commentary.
  • Natural Language Processing (NLP): The engine interprets the nuances of user questions, understanding intent and context to ensure answers are aligned with what the user really wants.
  • Content Synthesis: Rather than overwhelming users with raw data or endless links, the AI condenses information into concise, actionable insights—such as a step-by-step guide or key takeaways from authoritative sources.

Real-World Example:

A user asks, “What’s the best way to save for retirement?” Instead of listing 10 URLs, the AI synthesizes advice based on data from financial blogs, statistics, and expert opinions.

Takeaway: Content that’s concise, authoritative, and well-structured is more likely to be used by generative engines.

Strategies for Success: Optimizing with GEO

To succeed in GEO, you need a clear and actionable strategy. These steps will ensure your content is ready for AI-driven search engines:

Optimize for Structured Data

Structured data is like a roadmap for AI systems, helping them understand your content’s hierarchy and context. Use schema markup to make your content machine-readable. This can include:

  • FAQ schemas for common questions
  • How-To schemas for step-by-step guides
  • Product schemas for eCommerce listings.

Example:

A How-To schema could help AI engines identify the precise steps in your "How to Bake a Cake" guide, ensuring it’s featured in relevant responses.

Prioritize Scannability

AI engines process information quickly, so scannable content ensures your key points aren’t missed. Break up your text with clear headings (H2s, H3s), short paragraphs, and bullet points where necessary. Think of your content like a well-organized manual: it should be easy to skim but packed with value when read in detail. A guide on "Best Travel Tips" with clear sections like "Packing Hacks" or "Saving on Flights" is more likely to catch an AI’s attention than a dense block of text.

Focus on User Intent

Understanding user intent is at the heart of generative engine optimization. What are people truly asking, and how can your content provide the clearest, most helpful answers? Directly address their needs with actionable insights supported by evidence. For instance, if users frequently ask, "How can I improve my credit score?" provide a clear, step-by-step answer with supporting statistics or expert quotes to add credibility.

Build Authoritative Content

Authority is about trust. Incorporate references to credible sources and highlight your expertise, experience, and trustworthiness (E-E-A-T). For example, a financial blog discussing retirement plans should cite data from reputable sources like government agencies or financial institutions, making it more likely to be trusted by both users and AI engines.

Experiment with Content Formats

Different formats resonate with different users—and AI engines. While long-form guides are great for in-depth topics, micro-content like quick tips or infographics can be easier for AI to synthesize. For example, a travel website could pair a detailed destination guide with a quick "Top 5 Must-See Attractions" infographic, ensuring both formats appeal to AI-driven searches.

Performance Metrics: Measuring Generative Engine Optimization Success

Measuring success in GEO requires a shift from traditional SEO metrics. Instead of focusing solely on rankings, Generative Engine Optimization prioritizes how effectively your content serves AI-driven engines and user queries. Here are the key ways to evaluate your efforts:

Position-Adjusted Word Count (PAWC)

Think of this as a measure of how often your content becomes part of AI-generated responses. For example, if your article on "best pet care tips" is referenced multiple times in ChatGPT’s answers, your PAWC score reflects its frequency and prominence in high-performing outputs.

Subjective Impressions

This evaluates how well your content aligns with user intent. If someone asks an AI, "How can I improve my credit score?" and your guide is synthesized to answer their query clearly and effectively, your content is delivering on subjective impressions. Clarity, authority, and relevance are key here.

Tools for GEO Analytics

  • HubSpot’s AI Search Grader App: This tool helps identify gaps in your content’s AI performance, offering actionable insights for improvement.
  • Google Search Console (GSC): Monitor how often your content appears in search results influenced by AI-driven queries, tracking clicks and impressions to refine your strategy.

Understanding and optimizing these metrics ensures your content not only reaches audiences but actively contributes to the AI-driven answers they rely on.

Technical SEO for GEO: Best Practices

Technical SEO is the foundation that ensures your content is both accessible and valuable to generative AI engines. Without it, even the most well-written content can go unnoticed. Here’s how to optimize your technical SEO for GEO success:

Fast Load Times

Speed matters. Generative engines prioritize fast-loading websites because they improve user experience. Tools like Google PageSpeed Insights can help identify areas for improvement, such as optimizing images, leveraging browser caching, and reducing server response times. For instance, a 3-second delay in page load time can result in up to 40% of visitors abandoning your site.

Clear Site Architecture

Think of your website as a library, and site architecture as its organizational system. If a visitor (or AI crawler) can’t easily navigate through your content, its value diminishes. Use a logical structure with clear menus, breadcrumbs, and internal linking. For example, a travel site should categorize content by destination, trip type, or budget to make navigation intuitive.

Readable Formatting

Formatting is about making your content easy to digest for both humans and AI. Use proper HTML tags like H1 for titles, H2 for subheadings, and lists to break up dense information. For example, an FAQ page with clear headers and bullet points is far more accessible than one with long, unbroken paragraphs.

Unique Metadata

Metadata is your content’s first impression. Craft compelling meta titles and descriptions that accurately summarize the content while incorporating relevant keywords. For instance, instead of “Welcome to Our Blog,” use “5 Proven Strategies to Boost Your Credit Score in 2024” to provide value upfront and encourage clicks.

Brand Authority and Credibility in GEO

To succeed with Generative Engine Optimization, building trust and authority isn’t optional—it’s essential. Generative AI tools rely on credible, high-quality content to provide reliable answers, and your brand’s reputation plays a big role in this. Here’s how you can establish and maintain authority:

Consistent Branding

Think of branding as the voice that ties all your content together. Whether it’s a blog post, FAQ, or product description, your messaging should be cohesive and recognizable across platforms. For example, a fitness brand known for its approachable tone should carry that same tone into AI-referenced content like "how-to" workout guides or nutritional advice.

Credible Sources

Citing reputable sources adds weight to your content and increases its likelihood of being used by AI engines. For instance, if you’re discussing health topics, referencing studies from institutions like the Mayo Clinic or WHO instantly boosts your credibility. AI tools prioritize content that provides verifiable, high-quality information over opinion-based claims.

Unique Terminology

Stand out by using terminology that differentiates your brand while remaining clear and accessible. For example, if your skincare brand has coined a term for a specific regimen, define it clearly so it’s both unique and usable by AI systems. This helps establish expertise while ensuring your content isn’t lost in a sea of similar answers.

User-Centric Design

A seamless experience makes your content more appealing to users and AI. This means optimizing for readability—think clean layouts, simple language, and intuitive navigation. For instance, an educational site could use expandable sections for detailed answers, allowing users to find information quickly while AI engines can process the structured format efficiently.

Experimentation and Iteration in GEO

With Generative Engine Optimization, experimentation and iteration are not just optional—they’re essential. Success comes from testing new approaches, learning what works, and refining your strategy to align with the constant change in AI capabilities. Here’s how you can stay ahead:

A/B Testing

Experiment with different content formats, layouts, and structures to see what resonates most with AI engines. For example, test whether a list-based format or a detailed paragraph works better for your "How-to" guides. Use tools like Google Optimize to run these tests and gather actionable insights.

Review Content Performance

Analytics are your best friend when it comes to iteration. Track metrics like impressions, engagement, and how often your content is referenced in AI-generated responses. For instance, if your article on "best budgeting tips" isn’t being picked up, analyze its structure and compare it to similar content that is performing well.

Stay Informed

AI evolves rapidly, and so should your approach. Stay updated on advancements in generative models and how they process content. Subscribe to industry newsletters, follow updates from tools like OpenAI or Google Bard, and regularly assess your strategy to ensure it aligns with the latest trends.

By embracing these practices, you can keep your content relevant, impactful, and aligned with the demands of AI-driven search systems.

Integrating GEO Into Your Digital Strategy

GEO works best when it’s part of a well-rounded digital marketing strategy. It’s not a standalone solution—instead, it enhances the visibility and usability of your content in AI-driven search engines while complementing your existing efforts. Here’s how to integrate it effectively:

Audit Your Content

Start by identifying gaps where your content might be falling short in AI visibility. Are your articles structured for easy synthesis? Do they provide clear, actionable information? Use tools like HubSpot’s AI Search Grader App or Google Search Console to pinpoint areas for improvement and prioritize updates on high-traffic or high-value pages.

Collaborate Across Teams

Generative Engine Optimization isn’t just the responsibility of your SEO team—it requires alignment across multiple departments. For example, your content team should ensure readability and clarity, while your technical team focuses on structured data and load times. Regular collaboration helps ensure everyone is working towards the same goal: making your content AI-friendly.

Track and Optimize

Implement performance tracking to see how your GEO strategies are working. Monitor metrics like how often your content is referenced in AI-generated responses or shifts in organic traffic from AI-driven engines. For example, if a guide on “budget travel tips” suddenly sees more impressions from AI queries, analyze what’s working and apply those insights across your site.

By incorporating these steps, you’ll ensure GEO seamlessly integrates into your marketing strategy, driving better results and setting your content up for long-term success.

The Future of Search: AI Search Engines and GEO

AI-driven search is transforming how people find and consume information, and GEO is at the heart of this change. Here’s why understanding and adopting GEO is no longer optional:

Rapid Adoption

Generative AI tools are no longer fringe technologies—they’re mainstream. According to McKinsey, 65% of organizations now use generative AI tools to enhance operations, content production, and customer interactions. For example, AI-driven platforms like ChatGPT are being used to handle customer queries, create personalized marketing campaigns, and even develop product descriptions, underscoring their importance in everyday workflows.

Changing User Behaviour

People now expect fast, accurate, and actionable answers. Instead of sifting through a page of search results, users rely on AI tools to deliver concise, conversational responses. For instance, asking an AI, "What are the best ways to save for retirement?" yields a clear, actionable summary rather than a list of 10 links. This shift means content must be structured to meet these evolving expectations.

Business Implications

The competitive landscape is shifting. Companies that adopt GEO are better positioned to capture organic visibility within AI-driven platforms. For example, a financial services firm optimizing its blog with GEO principles can ensure its content is used in AI-generated advice, establishing authority and driving potential leads. Without GEO, businesses risk being overlooked entirely in this new ecosystem.

By embracing these trends, businesses can future-proof their digital strategies and ensure their content thrives in the AI-driven search landscape.

Bringing Generative Engine Optimization Into Focus

GEO is a transformative strategy for businesses looking to stay ahead in the rapidly evolving world of AI-driven search. By implementing the actionable steps outlined here, you can ensure your content isn’t just relevant but indispensable in AI-powered ecosystems. Whether it’s refining your technical SEO, optimizing for structured data, or building credibility, every step helps position your content as a trusted source for AI-driven tools.

Need help with GEO? Let us guide you through a free audit to optimize your content for AI-driven search.
excerpt: Master Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) with actionable tips to boost AI-driven visibility, build authority, and future-proof your content strategy.

title: How to Perform a Technical SEO Audit: A Step-by-Step Guide
content:
High-quality, user-focused content is an essential part of any SEO strategy. However, if your website could be faster, easier to navigate, or better structured, even the best content won’t bring you any traffic, or worse, it will make your users bounce before they read it. Optimizing your technical SEO through regular technical SEO audits improves your website’s functionality and helps rank on the first page of the SERP (Search Engine Results Page). If your site isn’t appearing on the front page, you’re missing out on valuable organic traffic.

This guide will walk you through each step of performing a comprehensive technical SEO audit, helping you enhance your site for search engines and users.

What is technical SEO?

Technical SEO is the part of SEO that focuses on the functionality and infrastructure of your website. By optimizing your site to be easier for search engines to crawl and index, your site is more likely to rank. Examples of technical SEO include improving page speed, checking robots.txt files, ensuring redirects work correctly, and enhancing mobile usability.

By addressing these aspects, technical SEO helps create a strong foundation for your website’s overall SEO performance, ensuring that content and design efforts are successful. A well-executed technical SEO strategy, including regular SEO audits, ensures search engines can fully access, understand, and rank your site effectively.

Technical SEO vs. on-page SEO vs. off-page SEO

Understanding the different components of search engine optimization (SEO) is crucial for developing a comprehensive SEO strategy. Technical SEO, on-page SEO, and off-page SEO each play distinct roles in improving your website’s visibility and performance in search engines.

Technical SEO focuses on the technical aspects of your website, such as site speed, mobile-friendliness, and XML sitemaps. These elements ensure that search engine crawlers can easily access, crawl, and index your site. By optimizing these technical factors, you create a solid foundation for your website’s overall SEO performance.

On-page SEO involves optimizing individual web pages to improve their relevance and ranking. This includes crafting unique and descriptive meta descriptions, titles, and content that naturally incorporate primary keywords. On-page SEO ensures that each page on your site is optimized for both search engines and users, enhancing its visibility in search results.

Off-page SEO refers to activities outside your website that impact its ranking. This includes building high-quality backlinks, engaging in social media marketing, and optimizing for local SEO. Off-page SEO helps establish your website’s authority and credibility, driving more traffic and improving its overall search engine rankings.

By integrating technical SEO, on-page SEO, and off-page SEO into your SEO strategy, you can create a well-rounded approach that maximizes your website’s potential to rank higher in search results.

Why is a technical SEO audit critical for ranking?

A technical SEO audit ensures search engines will see, crawl, and rank your website. Optimizing technical SEO ensures your site is ranking and bringing in traffic. With these optimizations, Google and other search engines may be able to find, crawl, and index your site.

Consider this: a study by Ahrefs found that 96.55% of content gets no traffic from Google. By performing an SEO audit, you can keep yourself in the top percentage of content that ranks and brings in traffic. Using a technical SEO audit checklist can help ensure that key aspects of technical SEO are not overlooked.

A technical SEO audit is vital for several reasons:

  • Ensures visibility: Search engines prioritize well-optimized, functional websites.
  • Improves user experience: A technically sound site leads to faster loading times and easier site navigation.
  • Adapts to updates: Auditing helps you stay current with search engine algorithm changes.

By regularly auditing your website, you can ensure your users have the best experience, continue to improve rankings, and outperform your competitors.

How do you do a technical SEO audit?

Step 1: Check your site's crawlability

The most important part of an SEO audit is analyzing your crawlability, which determines whether search engines can access your website’s content.

Using the URL inspection tool in Google Search Console can offer insights into webpage indexing status and potential issues.

Here’s how to check:

  1. Use a Crawling Tool: Tools like Screaming Frog, Sitebulb, or SEMrush can crawl your site and identify any blocked pages.
  2. Review Robots.txt: Ensure your robots.txt file isn’t inadvertently blocking essential pages.
  3. Check XML Sitemaps: Verify that your sitemap is up-to-date and correctly submitted to search engines.

Pro Tip:

Ensure your XML sitemap is clean and includes only indexable URLs.

Step 2: Assess site speed and performance

Page speed directly impacts user experience and rankings. Use these tools to analyze and improve your site’s performance:

  • Google PageSpeed Insights: Identify performance issues and receive actionable recommendations.
  • GTmetrix or WebPageTest: Measure your site’s loading time and identify bottlenecks.
  • Core Web Vitals: Focus on metrics like Largest Contentful Paint (LCP), First Input Delay (FID), and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS).

Additionally, using Google Analytics can provide detailed insights into site interactions, traffic sources, and user engagement metrics, which can significantly inform your SEO strategies and technical audits.

Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS)

Core Web Vitals are a set of metrics introduced by Google to measure the user experience of a website. These metrics are essential for technical SEO as they directly impact both user satisfaction and search engine rankings.

Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures the time it takes for the main content of a page to load. A fast LCP ensures that users can quickly see and interact with the primary content, reducing bounce rates and improving user engagement.

First Input Delay (FID) measures the time it takes for a page to become interactive. A low FID indicates that users can quickly interact with your site, such as clicking buttons or filling out forms, enhancing the overall user experience.

Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) measures the stability of a page’s layout as it loads. A low CLS ensures that elements on the page do not shift unexpectedly, preventing user frustration and improving the visual stability of your site.

To optimize these Core Web Vitals, focus on improving your site’s loading speed, reducing server response times, and ensuring that your layout remains stable during loading. By addressing these metrics, you can enhance the user experience and boost your search engine rankings.

Pro Tip:

  • Optimize images using compression tools like TinyPNG.
  • Minify CSS, JavaScript, and HTML.
  • Enable browser caching and use a Content Delivery Network (CDN).

Step 3: Analyze mobile-friendliness

With most web traffic coming from mobile devices, a mobile-friendly site is non-negotiable. To evaluate your site's mobile experience:

  1. Google's Mobile-Friendly Test: Test individual pages for mobile usability.
  2. Responsive Design Check: Ensure your site adapts seamlessly across different devices and screen sizes.
  3. Touch-Friendly Design: Verify that buttons and links are easy to tap.

Pro Tip:

Aim for a consistent user experience across your site's desktop and mobile versions.

Step 4: Fix broken links and redirects

Broken links and improper redirects can hurt your rankings and frustrate users.

Here's how to address these issues:

  1. Identify broken links: Use tools like Ahrefs or Screaming Frog to find 404 errors.
  2. Audit redirects: Check for 302 redirects and update them to 301 redirects where appropriate.
  3. Create a custom 404 page: Provide helpful navigation links to retain visitors.
  4. Optimize URL structure: Ensure URLs are clean, descriptive, and follow a logical hierarchy.

Key terms to know:

  • URL structure: The anatomy of how a particular URL looks.
  • 404 pages: The page a user sees when trying to reach a non-existent page on your site.
  • 301 redirects: A redirect from one page to another on your site.

Step 5: Evaluate on-page SEO elements

Technical SEO includes optimizing on-page elements for better visibility:

  • Meta Tags: Ensure title tags and meta descriptions are unique, descriptive, and within the character limit. Include primary keywords in titles and descriptions naturally. 
  • Header Tags: Structure your content logically using H1, H2, and H3 tags.
  • Canonical Tags: A canonical tag is a way to tell search engines what the most representative page on your site is. Prevent duplicate content issues by implementing canonical URLs.

Step 6: Optimize for HTTPS and security

Search engines favour secure websites. Here's how to check:

  1. HTTPS Implementation: Ensure your site uses HTTPS with a valid SSL certificate.
  2. Fix Mixed Content: Address any pages that load HTTP and HTTPS resources.
  3. Enable Security Features: Implement features like HSTS to enhance security.

Step 7: Monitor and fix indexing issues

Ensure search engines can index all critical pages on your site:

  1. Google Search Console: Identify pages with indexing errors or warnings.
  2. Noindex Tags: Verify that critical pages are not mistakenly tagged as "noindex."
  3. Duplicate Content: Resolve duplicate content issues with canonicalization or 301 redirects.

Pro Tip:

Regularly check your coverage report in Google Search Console to spot and fix issues promptly.

Step 8: Review structured data

Structured data helps search engines understand your content and display rich snippets. To audit and optimize:

  • Validate with Google's Rich Results Test: Identify errors in your schema markup.
  • Use JSON-LD Format: Ensure your structured data follows best practices.
  • Add Relevant Markup: Implement schema types like FAQ, Breadcrumb, and Product, depending on your site's content.

Step 9: Fix image issues

Images are critical for visual appeal but can slow down your site if not optimized. Address these common image issues:

  1. Incorrect dimensions: Resize images to fit their display dimensions.
  2. Large file sizes: Compress images without sacrificing quality.
  3. Incorrect formats: Use modern formats like WebP for faster load times.
  4. Missing alt text: Add descriptive alt text to improve accessibility and SEO.

Using Google Search Console for insights

Google Search Console (GSC) is an invaluable tool for gaining insights into your website’s performance in search engines. It provides a wealth of data that can help you identify and fix technical SEO issues, ultimately improving your site’s visibility and rankings.

With GSC, you can monitor search queries, impressions, clicks, and average position, giving you a clear picture of how your site is performing in search results. Additionally, GSC offers detailed reports on crawl errors, sitemap submissions, and indexing issues, allowing you to address any technical problems that may be hindering your site’s performance.

To make the most of GSC, regularly review the Performance report to understand which keywords and pages are driving traffic to your site. Use the Coverage report to identify and fix any crawl errors or indexing issues. Additionally, submit your XML sitemaps through GSC to ensure that search engines can easily find and index your content.

By leveraging the insights provided by Google Search Console, you can make data-driven decisions to optimize your website for better search engine rankings and user experience.

Create an action plan for after your technical SEO audit

After conducting a technical SEO audit, it’s essential to create a detailed action plan to address the identified issues. This plan should prioritize the most critical issues and provide a clear timeline for implementation.

Start by listing all the issues identified during the audit, such as crawl errors, broken links, and duplicate content. Prioritize these issues based on their impact on search engine rankings and user experience. For example, fixing crawl errors and broken links should take precedence over optimizing meta descriptions and titles.

Next, outline specific actions to address each issue. For instance, if you identified slow site speed, your action plan might include optimizing images, minifying CSS and JavaScript, and enabling browser caching. If mobile-friendliness is an issue, consider implementing responsive design and testing your site on various devices.

Create a timeline for implementing these actions, starting with the highest-priority issues. Set realistic deadlines and allocate resources accordingly. Regularly review your progress and adjust your plan as needed to ensure that all issues are addressed promptly.

By creating a comprehensive action plan, you can systematically address technical SEO issues and improve your website’s performance and search engine rankings.

Interpreting findings and prioritizing technical SEO issues

Interpreting the findings of a technical SEO audit is crucial for prioritizing and addressing the most critical issues. Start by categorizing the issues based on their impact on search engine rankings and user experience.

High-priority issues, such as crawl errors and broken links, should be addressed first, as they can significantly affect your site’s visibility and usability. These issues can prevent search engines from indexing your content and frustrate users, leading to higher bounce rates.

Medium-priority issues, such as optimizing meta descriptions and titles, should be addressed next. While these issues may not have as immediate an impact as crawl errors, they are still essential for improving your site’s relevance and click-through rates in search results.

Low-priority issues, such as minor layout adjustments or less critical content updates, can be addressed last. While these issues may enhance the overall user experience, they are less likely to have a significant impact on search engine rankings.

By prioritizing the most critical issues first, you can ensure that your website is optimized for better search engine rankings and user experience. Regularly review and update your action plan to address new issues as they arise and maintain your site’s performance over time.

Building a foundation for search engine optimization success

Performing a technical SEO audit may seem daunting, but breaking it into manageable steps simplifies the process. Regular audits help maintain your site’s performance, adapt to algorithm updates, and provide a seamless user experience. At a minimum, a technically sound website should be secure, quick to load, easy to crawl, have clear and actionable navigation, and not contain duplicate links or content.

Start your audit now and watch the impact of your hard work increase your rankings—because a technically sound website is the foundation of SEO success. Integrating insights from multiple tools can guide agencies in refining their SEO strategies to achieve the best possible results.

title: SEO Maintenance Checklist for 2025: Stay Ahead All Year
content:
SEO is not a “set it and forget it” strategy. Search engines continuously evolve, and businesses must adapt to remain competitive. Consistent SEO maintenance ensures your website performs well, meets user needs, and aligns with search engine updates. Incorporating SEO into your monthly business strategy can secure long-term growth, boost organic traffic, and improve search engine rankings.

This blog outlines what entails and provides you with a comprehensive SEO maintenance checklist for daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual tasks to help you stay ahead in the ever-changing search environment.

Why do you need monthly SEO maintenance?

Improving your search rankings isn’t accidental—it results from deliberate, ongoing efforts. SEO maintenance is akin to exercising for your health: consistency is key to reaping the benefits. Creating content that reflects current trends and topics is essential for engaging audiences and achieving SEO success.

Understanding SEO basics

SEO, or Search Engine Optimization, is the art and science of enhancing a website’s visibility in search engine results pages (SERPs). By optimizing various elements of your site, you can improve its ranking and attract more organic traffic. Understanding the basics of SEO is crucial for anyone looking to boost their online presence.

Search engines like Google use complex algorithms to crawl, index, and rank websites based on factors such as relevance, authority, and user experience. SEO involves fine-tuning your website’s content, structure, and technical aspects to align with these algorithms and improve your chances of ranking higher in search results.

Here are some key concepts to grasp:

  • Keyword research: This involves identifying the most relevant and high-traffic keywords related to your business or niche. Effective keyword research helps you understand what your audience is searching for and how to target those queries.
  • On-page optimization: This includes optimizing elements like title tags, meta descriptions, headings, and content to make your website more search engine-friendly. Proper on-page SEO ensures that search engines can easily understand and index your content.
  • Technical SEO: This focuses on improving the technical aspects of your website, such as page speed, mobile responsiveness, and XML sitemaps. Technical SEO enhances user experience and helps search engine crawlers navigate your site more efficiently.
  • Link building: Building high-quality backlinks from other reputable websites increases your site’s authority and improves its ranking. Link building is a critical component of off-page SEO.

By mastering these SEO basics, you can lay a strong foundation for your website’s search engine optimization, ultimately boosting its visibility, traffic, and conversion rates.

Key benefits of monthly SEO maintenance for organic traffic:

Monitor progress: Evaluate short-term and long-term goals with actionable metrics. Utilize Google Search Console's tools to track and improve SEO performance by identifying and resolving crawling and indexing errors, and analyzing internal links to optimize site structure.

Adapt to changes: Respond promptly to algorithm updates and industry trends to retain rankings.

Prevent issues: Identify and resolve technical or content-related problems before they affect your visibility.

Stay competitive: Maintain or improve rankings in a fluctuating search engine landscape.

By breaking tasks into snackable chunks, this SEO maintenance checklist enables a steady, effective, and proactive approach organic marketing.

Setting up SEO foundations

Establishing solid SEO foundations is essential for any website aiming to improve its search engine ranking and attract more visitors. Here are the key steps to set up your SEO foundations effectively:

  1. Conduct keyword research: Start by identifying the most relevant and high-traffic keywords related to your business or niche. Use tools like Google Keyword Planner, Ahrefs, or SEMrush to find keywords that your target audience is searching for. This will help you create content that meets their needs and ranks well in search results.
  2. Set up Google Search Console: Create a Google Search Console account to monitor your website’s performance in search engine rankings. The Search Console provides valuable insights into how Google views your site, including indexing status, search queries, and potential issues that need addressing.
  3. Install Google Analytics: Set up Google Analytics to track your website’s traffic, user behavior, and conversion rates. This tool helps you understand how visitors interact with your site, which pages are most popular, and where improvements are needed.
  4. Optimize website structure: Ensure your website has a clear and organized structure with easy navigation and a mobile-friendly design. A well-structured site enhances user experience and makes it easier for search engine bots to crawl and index your content.
  5. Create high-quality content: Develop a content strategy focused on creating high-quality, engaging, and informative content that targets your identified keywords. Regularly updating your site with fresh content not only attracts visitors but also signals to search engines that your site is active and relevant.

By setting up these SEO foundations, you can create a robust base for your website’s search engine optimization, improving its visibility, traffic, and conversion rates.

Setting realistic SEO strategy goals

To succeed in SEO, it’s crucial to establish clear, measurable, and actionable goals.

Understanding and analyzing SEO strategies is essential when setting realistic goals. By gaining insights into top-performing keywords, backlinks, and overall organic performance, you can create a more effective plan.

Steps to define SEO goals with keyword research:

  1. Set SMART objectives: Your goals should be Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. For example, aim to increase organic traffic by 20% in six months or achieve first-page rankings for high-value keywords.
  2. Understand SEO timeframes: SEO results take time; manage stakeholder expectations by explaining the long-term nature of improvements.

Align goals with business objectives: Ensure SEO efforts support broader business goals, such as lead generation or e-commerce sales.

Keyword research and planning

Keyword research and planning are the cornerstones of any successful SEO strategy. Here’s how to conduct effective keyword research and planning:

  1. Identify seed keywords: Begin by brainstorming a list of seed keywords related to your business or niche. These are the basic terms that describe your products, services, or content.
  2. Use keyword research tools: Utilize tools like Google Keyword Planner, Ahrefs, or SEMrush to expand your list of keywords. These tools provide data on search volume, competition, and related keywords, helping you identify the most valuable terms to target.
  3. Analyze competitors: Examine your competitors’ websites to see which keywords they are targeting. Identify gaps in their keyword strategy that you can exploit. Tools like Ahrefs and SEMrush can help you uncover your competitors’ top-performing keywords.
  4. Create a keyword list: Compile a comprehensive list of relevant keywords and phrases that you want to target. Group them into categories based on their relevance and search intent.
  5. Prioritize keywords: Prioritize your keywords based on factors like search volume, competition, and relevance to your business. Focus on a mix of high-traffic, competitive keywords and long-tail keywords that are more specific and less competitive.

By conducting thorough keyword research and planning, you can develop a targeted SEO strategy that drives more traffic and conversions to your website.

The 2025 SEO maintenance checklist

SEO maintenance checklist — daily tasks

  1. Stay updated: Read reputable SEO blogs like Moz, Search Engine Journal, and SEMrush to stay informed about algorithm updates and best practices.
  2. Monitor key metrics:
    • Use Google Analytics to track traffic fluctuations and identify any unusual drops.
    • Use Google Search Console to identify indexing issues, ranking drops, or crawl errors.
  3. Spot emerging trends: Utilize tools like Google Trends to analyze shifts in search behavior and identify potential new keyword opportunities.
  4. Diagnose immediate issues:
    • Analyze sudden drops in traffic or rankings to find and address potential issues.
    • Address errors flagged by monitoring tools, such as broken links or server issues.

SEO maintenance checklist — weekly tasks

  1. Review progress toward goals: Analyze your website’s weekly performance, including organic traffic and engagement metrics, against your monthly and annual objectives.
  2. Implement minor adjustments: Optimize meta descriptions and titles for underperforming pages. Update internal links to improve navigation and keyword relevance. Conduct annual internal link analyses to enhance site navigation and link equity for under-linked pages.
  3. Technical SEO check: Ensure the website is mobile-responsive, optimize site speed, and verify HTTPS security protocols.
  4. Content improvements: Refresh or expand blog posts that are showing declining performance or nearing high-ranking positions.

SEO maintenance checklist — monthly tasks

  1. Monthly KPI reporting:
    • Organic traffic: Review patterns in traffic sources and identify spikes or dips.
    • Keyword rankings: Compare current rankings for priority keywords to previous months.
    • Conversions: Measure how organic traffic contributes to goals like sales or lead generation.
    • Backlink profile: Use tools like Ahrefs or SEMrush to evaluate the quality and quantity of backlinks. Address toxic links promptly.
  2. Perform in-depth audits:
    • Broken links: Identify and fix internal and external broken links to maintain a seamless user experience.
    • Keyword analysis: Conduct gap analyses to discover opportunities where competitors rank higher and update your targeting strategy.
    • Content review: Refresh underperforming pages and blogs, optimizing for intent and relevance.
  3. Enhance technical SEO:
    • Review Core Web Vitals metrics such as Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS).
    • Resolve crawling and indexing errors flagged in Google Search Console.
    • Ensure structured data is up-to-date and properly implemented.
  4. Plan ahead:
    • Identify seasonal opportunities and strategize for content that aligns with upcoming trends.
    • Develop campaigns that reflect both SEO and marketing objectives for the next month.

SEO maintenance checklist — quarterly tasks

  1. Conduct comprehensive audits:
    • Technical Audit: Address duplicate content, schema markup issues, and outdated sitemaps.
    • Cannibalization Analysis: Check for multiple pages targeting the same keywords and consolidate when necessary.
  2. Seasonal strategy updates:
    • Update landing pages for upcoming holidays, sales, or events.
    • Optimize local SEO elements like Google Business Profile for accurate information and promotions.
  3. Competitor analysis:
    • Use competitive analysis tools to identify keywords or strategies driving traffic to competitors.
    • Incorporate insights into your content and link-building strategies.

SEO maintenance checklist — annual tasks

  1. Comprehensive content audit:
    • Evaluate each page’s performance based on traffic, engagement, and conversions.
    • Decide whether to update, merge, or remove low-performing content.
    • Identify evergreen content that can be repurposed or promoted further.
  2. Set yearly goals:
    • Use insights from the previous year to inform new objectives and benchmarks.
    • Stay aligned with evolving SEO trends, such as changes in search intent or user behavior.
  3. Evaluate overall SEO strategy:
    • Assess the effectiveness of your tools and platforms.
    • Identify areas for investment, such as content creation, link-building, or technical enhancements.

Technical SEO maintenance checklist

Technical SEO is a critical component of any SEO strategy, ensuring that your website is optimized for search engines and provides a seamless user experience. Here’s a comprehensive technical SEO checklist to follow:

  1. Page speed optimization: Ensure your website loads quickly, ideally in less than 3 seconds. Use tools like Google PageSpeed Insights to identify and fix issues that may be slowing down your site. Faster loading times improve user experience and search engine rankings.
  2. Mobile responsiveness: Make sure your website is mobile-friendly and responsive. With the majority of users accessing the web via mobile devices, a mobile-optimized site is crucial for both user experience and search engine rankings.
  3. XML sitemap: Create and submit an XML sitemap to help search engines understand your website’s structure and content. This ensures that all your important pages are indexed and can be found in search results.
  4. Robots.txt: Optimize your robots.txt file to guide search engine bots on which pages to crawl and index. This helps prevent indexing of duplicate or low-value content.
  5. SSL encryption: Install an SSL certificate to secure your website and protect user data. HTTPS is a ranking factor for search engines, and a secure site builds trust with your visitors.
  6. Website architecture: Ensure your website has a clear and organized structure with easy navigation and minimal broken links. A well-structured site enhances user experience and helps search engine crawlers navigate your content more efficiently.
  7. Meta tags: Optimize your meta tags, including title tags, meta descriptions, and header tags. Well-crafted meta tags improve search engine ranking and click-through rates by providing clear and relevant information to both search engines and users.

By following this technical SEO checklist, you can ensure your website is optimized for search engines, improving its visibility, traffic, and conversion rates.

Pro tips for successful SEO maintenance

  1. Invest in the right tools: Use platforms like Ahrefs, Moz, or SEMrush for advanced insights and analysis.
  2. Optimize for voice search: Incorporate conversational, long-tail keywords to capture voice search traffic.
  3. Leverage social signals: Encourage content sharing and monitor brand mentions to strengthen off-page SEO.
  4. Build quality backlinks: Focus on earning backlinks from authoritative, relevant sources.
  5. Stay flexible: Be ready to adapt strategies based on new data or industry developments.

SEO maintenance is vital for staying competitive in 2025 and beyond. By following this detailed SEO maintenance checklist, businesses can achieve consistent growth, respond to changes effectively, and ensure their website remains a valuable asset. Make SEO a priority—your rankings, traffic, and customers will thank you.
author: sketch

title: SEO KPIs 2025: Key Metrics to Boost Your Performance
content:
Understanding which Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to monitor can seem overwhelming, but identifying and regularly tracking the right SEO metrics is essential for optimizing strategies, measuring success, and achieving long-term growth. This guide breaks down the key SEO KPIs to monitor, why they matter, and how to track them effectively.

Why tracking SEO KPIs is essential

SEO metrics provide valuable insights into how your website is performing and help you:

  • Understand which strategies are working.
  • Identify areas for improvement.
  • Optimize for better rankings, traffic, and conversions.
  • Demonstrate the ROI of your SEO efforts.

By monitoring these KPIs, you’ll make informed decisions and ensure your SEO strategy aligns with your goals.

How often should you track SEO KPIs 2025?

Tracking frequency depends on the metric and your goals:

  • Monthly reports: These provide a comprehensive view of progress and help identify trends, as most SEO changes take time to show results. Key metrics to monitor include organic search traffic, domain authority, conversion rates, and visibility on the search engine results page (SERP).
  • Weekly or bi-weekly checks: Metrics like bounce rate, keyword rankings, and crawl errors should be monitored more frequently to catch and resolve issues promptly.
  • Year-over-year (YoY) and seasonal comparisons: Industries with seasonal variations benefit from analyzing year-to-year trends to understand long-term performance.

Understanding SEO metrics

What are SEO KPIs?

SEO KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) are metrics used to evaluate the success of a search engine optimization (SEO) campaign. They help track, measure, and analyze the effectiveness of SEO efforts, providing insights into website performance, user behavior, and search engine rankings. Common KPIs include metrics such as organic traffic, keyword rankings, search engine results page (SERP), click-through rate (CTR), conversion rate, and domain authority. By monitoring these indicators, businesses can gauge how well their SEO strategies are working and identify areas for improvement.

How to choose what to track

Choosing the right SEO KPIs to track depends on the current state of your website and your business goals. For instance, if increasing organic traffic is a primary objective, then prioritizing KPIs such as organic traffic, keyword rankings, and click-through rate would be crucial. Additionally, consider the scalability and feasibility of tracking and measuring each KPI within your resource constraints. Focus on actionable KPIs and provide insights that can drive meaningful improvements to your SEO strategy. This targeted approach ensures that your SEO efforts are efficient and effective.

Setting up your SEO KPI tracking

Setting up your SEO KPI tracking is a crucial step in measuring the success of your search engine optimization efforts. Here’s how:

  1. Set clear SEO goals Define what you want to achieve through your SEO efforts, such as increasing organic traffic, improving search engine rankings, or boosting conversion rates. Having clear goals helps you focus on the right metrics.
  2. Choose your metrics Key metrics to track include:
    • Organic traffic: The number of visitors arriving via search engines.
    • Keyword rankings: Your website’s position in SERPs for specific keywords.
    • Conversion rates: The percentage of visitors completing desired actions, such as purchases or sign-ups.
    • Bounce rate: The percentage of visitors leaving your site without further interaction.
    • Average session duration: The time visitors spend on your website.
  3. Review and reflect regularly Regularly review your metrics to identify areas for improvement, track progress, and optimize your strategy. Use tools like Google Analytics and Google Search Console for tracking.

Key SEO KPIs 2025

Organic traffic

What it is: Organic traffic measures the number of visitors who arrive at your website via search engines. It reflects the effectiveness of your site in attracting users without paid advertisements.

Why it’s important: Growth in organic traffic signals that your keyword optimization and content strategies are driving results, improving visibility and engagement.

How to track it: Use tools like Google Analytics 4 (GA4) to monitor organic sessions and analyze trends over time. Evaluate the sources of traffic and the keywords bringing users to your site.

Keyword rankings

What it is: Keyword ranking is a critical factor in determining a website’s position on the search engine results page (SERP) for specific keywords. High rankings on the SERP improve visibility and attract potential visitors.

Why it’s important: Tracking keyword rankings helps you measure the success of your optimization efforts and identify opportunities for improvement or new keyword targeting.

How to track it: Utilize tools like SEMrush, Ahrefs, or Google Search Console to monitor keyword positions and assess how they fluctuate over time. Focus on improving rankings for high-intent and competitive keywords.

Click-through rate (CTR)

What it is: CTR measures the percentage of users who click on your website link after seeing it in search results. Creating engaging metadata that encourages users to click on your search result can significantly impact your organic CTR and the perceived relevance of your webpage to users' search intent.

Why it’s important: A higher CTR indicates that your search snippets are compelling and relevant, leading to increased traffic and engagement.

How to track it: Google Search Console provides data on CTR for individual pages and keywords. Experiment with different meta descriptions and titles to optimize this metric.

Bounce rate and dwell time

What it is: Bounce rate measures the percentage of visitors who leave your site after viewing only one page. Dwell time tracks how long users stay engaged on your site before leaving.

Why it’s important: A high bounce rate or low dwell time often indicates issues like irrelevant content or poor user experience, which can hurt rankings. Understanding search intent is crucial, as aligning your SEO content writing with user expectations can significantly improve these metrics.

How to track it: Use GA4 to analyze these metrics. Improve by providing valuable content, optimizing navigation, and ensuring fast load speeds.

Core web vitals

What it is: Core Web Vitals assess the performance of your site’s loading speed, interactivity, and visual stability. These include:

  • Largest Contentful Paint (LCP): Measures loading time.
  • First Input Delay (FID): Tracks interactivity.
  • Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS): Ensures visual stability.

Why it’s important: These metrics directly impact user experience and are critical ranking factors in Google’s algorithm, contributing significantly to SEO success.

How to track it: Use tools like PageSpeed Insights and Lighthouse to monitor Core Web Vitals. Address issues by optimizing images, reducing server response times, and refining code.

Backlink profile

What it is: A backlink profile evaluates the quantity and quality of inbound links pointing to your site. High-quality backlinks indicate authority and trustworthiness.

Why it’s important: Strong backlinks boost domain authority and improve rankings, signaling to search engines that your content is valuable.

How to track it: Tools like Ahrefs and Moz can analyze your backlink profile. Build links through content creation, outreach, and partnerships with reputable websites.

Conversion rate (CR)

What it is: Conversion rate measures the percentage of visitors who complete desired actions, such as making purchases or signing up for newsletters.

Why it’s important: This KPI connects your SEO efforts to business outcomes, ensuring strategies deliver tangible value.

How to Track It: Use GA4’s event tracking to measure conversions. Optimize CTAs, landing pages, and user flows to boost this metric.

Mobile usability

What it is: Mobile usability evaluates how well your website performs on mobile devices, including responsiveness and navigation.

Why it’s important: With mobile-first indexing, a mobile-friendly site is critical for ranking and user experience.

How to track it: Tools like Google’s Mobile-Friendly Test highlight issues and provide recommendations. Focus on responsive design, fast load speeds, and streamlined navigation.

Local SEO metrics

What it is: Local SEO metrics include Google Business Profile impressions and local keyword rankings. These KPIs are vital for businesses targeting specific geographic areas.

Why it’s important: Improving these metrics enhances visibility for local searches and drives foot traffic or regional engagement.

How to track it: Monitor performance through Google Business Profile Insights and ensure accurate, up-to-date information on your profile.

Crawl errors

What it is: Crawl errors occur when search engines cannot access specific pages on your site, preventing them from being indexed properly.

Why it’s important: Unresolved crawl errors can hurt rankings by making valuable content invisible to search engines.

How to track it: Google Search Console highlights crawl errors. Fix issues like broken links, incorrect redirects, and blocked resources promptly.

Return on investment (ROI)

What it is: ROI measures the profitability of your SEO strategy by comparing revenue to costs. It reflects the financial success of your efforts.

Why it’s important: Tracking ROI demonstrates the value of SEO and helps allocate resources effectively.

How to track it: Use advanced analytics tools or GA4 to compare costs versus revenue. Identify which strategies yield the highest returns.

SEO strategy and planning

Setting clear SEO goals

Setting clear SEO goals is essential for developing an effective SEO strategy. Goals should be specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART). For example, a goal might be to increase organic traffic by 20% within the next six months or to improve keyword rankings for specific search queries. By setting clear goals, businesses can focus their SEO efforts, prioritize tasks, and measure progress toward achieving their objectives. This structured approach not only enhances the efficiency of your SEO campaign but also ensures that your efforts are aligned with your overall business strategy.

Tools to monitor SEO KPIs with Google Search Console

  • Google Analytics 4 (GA4): Track traffic, user engagement, and conversions. 
  • Google Search Console: Monitor keyword rankings, search engine results page (SERP), CTR, and crawl errors. 
  • SEMrush, Ahrefs, Moz: Analyze backlinks, keyword performance, and domain authority. 
  • PageSpeed Insights and Lighthouse: Optimize Core Web Vitals and technical performance. 

Google Business Profile insights: Track local SEO performance.

Using SEO tools like Semrush and Ahrefs is essential for evaluating a website’s health and tracking performance metrics. Monitoring these essential SEO KPIs in 2025 is critical for refining your strategies, improving your rankings, and driving meaningful results. By consistently analyzing your performance, you’ll stay competitive and ensure your SEO efforts deliver tangible outcomes.
